text: The surgeon performs a robotic repair of a hiatal hernia using biologic mesh and a toupee fundoplication. The hernia sac is opened, starting from the left side and proceeding circumferentially towards the right crura, revealing the attenuated phrenoesophageal ligament.

The surgeon continues dissection in the posterior mediastinum, opening the gastrohepatic ligament to identify the caudate lobe, inferior vena cava, and right crura. The dissection progresses along the right cruse, creating a plane between the right crura and the hernia sac contents, enhancing visualization of the right side structures and aiming to identify the convergence of the right and left crura at the base.

The surgeon mobilizes and frees up the attachments to the left crus, carefully dissecting to avoid damage to the short gastric vessels. The hemicephageal ligament and hernia sac are visibly attenuated during this process.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of attachments to the left crura, facilitating the creation of a retrosophageal window. An instrument is visible emerging from the retrosophageal window at the left upper side, where panrethrin is introduced to retract the esophagus. Dissection continues to enlarge the retrosophageal window, followed by closure of the posterior crural defect, with careful preservation of the peritoneum overlying both crura.

The surgeon places multiple figure-of-eight sutures using 0 Ethibond, ensuring enough space for a 5mm instrument next to the esophagus to prevent impingement. The hernia sac is fully mobilized and freed to avoid interference with the fundoplication. An anterior choroplasty may require additional anterior stitches, and a biological mesh is utilized.

The surgeon introduces a mesh and places it in a reverse letter C configuration, securing it with U-stitches to the underlying structures. A suture is placed on the prominent portion of the freed-up cardia, positioning it in a retro-sophageal manner for fundoplication. Due to concerns about tension, a posterior toupee fundoplication is performed, involving multiple sutures to achieve a 180-degree wrap of the esophagus around the cardia.
The surgeon removes the Penrose drain and inspects the hernia closure site. The mesh is positioned and tucked away securely in the proper location.
